title: --userns=keep-id freezes Podman when starting devcontainer from MS
body:
### Issue Description

Podman hangs, when running a docker image.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Install latest WSL on Windows 11
2. Run following command: 

```
podman run --userns=keep-id --user 1000:1000 -v $PWD:$PWD --workdir=$PWD --privileged -ti --network host mcr.microsoft.com/devcontainers/cpp:latest
```



### Describe the results you received

podman hangs, nothing happens

### Describe the results you expected

I am in the docker container started via podman

comments.body: what is the solution? I dont see anything there solved
you can try configuring fuse-overlayfs.  It has some disadvantages as FUSE is slower, but we don't need to chown the image when a different user namespace is used

Maybe I will try it out. I actually deleted podman and switched to docker, because there it just works. I just wanted to help awareness of this issue. Should be reproducable with a fresh system quite easily.

So how does `--userns=keep-id --user 1000:1000` work exactly with Docker?

The problem you are seeing is that idmapped mounts are not supported from a user namespaces so they can't work in a rootless environment.   To make it "just work" with Podman just run as root, then it is equivalent to what you are doing with Docker.

Correct rootless docker would have the same issue.

I always thought that we should allow users to setup default user namespace, so that images are pull in the --userns=keep-id user namespace rather then the default.

Right now we pull within the default userns and then when you run a container we need to chown all files on an overlay with the keep-id userns.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> To make it "just work" with Podman just run as root, then it is equivalent to what you are doing with Docker.

Unfortunately some software just hates running with uid `0` :(

> > To make it "just work" with Podman just run as root, then it is equivalent to what you are doing with Docker.
> 
> Unfortunately some software just hates running with uid `0` :(

that was referring to the user running Podman, not the ID inside the container.  root can use idmapped mounts, so there is no price to pay when you set up a different mapping
Ah I understand now, sorry for the mis-read. Sorry for the noise, I see this is the wrong issue for my particular problem.
